summary: Gruber reviews the Apple Watch Ultra after a week of daily wear, finding it a compelling new expression of the Apple Watch that fills a gap for users wanting something bigger and bolder. He argues the Ultra feels like a different size class entirely from the Series models, not just a scaled-up version, with the flat display and Action button creating a genuinely different experience. The titanium construction keeps it surprisingly light despite its visual heft, and battery life effectively doubles the Series models. He draws a tempered analogy to the iPad's relationship with iPhone, suggesting the Ultra feels capable enough to be a standalone device but is held back by WatchOS being designed primarily for the smaller Series watches. Gruber concludes that while it's too big and expensive for most people, the Ultra represents what Apple Watch can be when unshackled from the two-size paradigm.
key_points: The Ultra is a genuinely different size class from Series models, not just bigger — the flat display makes apps like Calculator and the QWERTY keyboard actually usable, creating a qualitatively different experience.
The Action button is the most significant functional difference, being the first user-configurable hardware button on Apple Watch and opening up third-party customization possibilities.
Titanium construction allows 76% more battery in a case only 34% larger by volume, delivering roughly double the battery life of Series models.
Gruber argues WatchOS is holding the Ultra back, much as iOS hamstrings the iPad — the hardware is capable enough to function as a standalone cellular device, but the software doesn't let it.
Watch size anxiety is mostly self-consciousness — the Ultra looks big but works on even smaller wrists, and people should buy the watch that makes them happy.
money_quotes: If Apple Watch Ultra were the first (and thus only) Apple Watch, people would lose their minds.
Your watch should make you happy every time you look at it.
It's unabashedly a computer on your wrist.
Switching between a 45mm Series 8 and the Apple Watch Ultra feels different, not just bigger.
A week wearing an Apple Watch Ultra makes me wish the Series models had an Action button too. Why not? Three total buttons is not a lot of buttons for a digital watch.
The orange accent color of the Action button and Digital Crown are a delightfully opinionated touch.
It almost feels more like having an adorable little iPhone Nano strapped to my wrist than a huge Apple Watch.
You want to cut down on your screen time? Cut down your cell phone to the size of a large watch.
key_insight: The Apple Watch Ultra isn't just a bigger Apple Watch — it's a genuinely different device class that feels capable of being a standalone computer on your wrist, but WatchOS won't let it reach that potential.
